effect_mechanism: WH structures increase agricultural water availability, enabling crop intensification and expanding the cropping season. This directly increases farm income and food production for poor smallholders. In pastoral systems, livestock watering points reduce dry-season livestock mortality, protecting household assets. Benefits are realised quickly (1-3 years) compared to tree-based NbS.
conditionality: Poverty reduction effect requires that farmers have secure land tenure to invest in structure maintenance and can access markets for surplus production enabled by improved water. Social equity dimensions (who controls pond access) need explicit attention.
economic_value_range: {"low_usd_ha_yr":30,"high_usd_ha_yr":400,"source_note":"Low: subsistence-scale water availability improvement from gully plugs and bunds (~$30/ha/yr income equivalent). High: farm pond enabling double-cropping or high-value horticulture (~$400/ha/yr). Ahmed et al. (2023) monetise crop pollination and forage production benefits in USD. Terencio et al. (2017) calculate investment cost ratios in EUR/m3 storage."}
justification: Shadeed et al. (2019) explicitly construct a water poverty index and WH suitability map, confirming WH is most valuable in high water-poverty areas. Mahmoud et al. (2014) target the poorest drought-prone zones for RWH in Ethiopia. [archetype: Quick_returns archetype: construction costs recovered within 1-3 seasons from improved yield stability and expanded irrigation. Most accessible economic archetype for poor smallholders without access to patient capital.]

effect_mechanism: WH structures directly bridge the gap between crop water demand and rainfall supply. Farm ponds store wet-season runoff for dry-season irrigation. Check dams raise groundwater tables, enabling dry-season cropping from wells. Contour bunding and tied ridging retain in-situ soil moisture, extending effective growing season and reducing yield gaps from mid-season dry spells.
conditionality: Effect proportional to storage volume relative to command area. Requires functional distribution infrastructure (pipes, canals) for stored water to reach crops. Evaporation losses (up to 30-50% in hot arid environments) reduce effective storage. Harka et al. (2020) explicitly model evaporation as a barrier in arid contexts.
economic_value_range: {"low_usd_ha_yr":50,"high_usd_ha_yr":500,"source_note":"Range reflects value of water made available: from subsistence supplemental irrigation to commercial horticulture. Terencio et al. (2017) calculate EUR/m3 storage and irrigation benefit ratios."}
justification: This is the fundamental purpose of water harvesting investment. Confirmed across all 85 stocktake papers as the primary intended outcome. Quantified by Singh et al. (2016), Alene et al. (2022), and Bera & Mukhopadhyay (2023) through runoff potential and demand matching analysis. [archetype: This is the core economic driver for WH investment — direct crop production value from water made available.]

effect_mechanism: Check dams and gully plugs trap sediment, reducing soil loss from active gullies. Contour bunding and terracing break slope length, reducing erosive runoff velocity and soil detachment. Percolation tanks increase infiltration, reducing surface flow erosive power. WH structures collectively shift the water balance from erosive surface flow toward storage and infiltration.
conditionality: Sediment trapping requires periodic de-silting to maintain storage capacity; neglected structures lose function within 5-10 years. Terracing effectiveness depends on correct bund height and spacing for local slope and rainfall intensity.
economic_value_range: {"low_usd_ha_yr":15,"high_usd_ha_yr":120,"source_note":"Avoided soil loss valued at replacement fertiliser cost. Singh et al. (2023) quantify RUSLE-based soil loss and watershed prioritisation. Al-Shamiri & Ziadat (2012) conduct monetary cost-benefit analysis of erosion control through WH."}
justification: Singh et al. (2023) explicitly apply RUSLE modelling to prioritise WH sites for soil loss prevention. Zhang et al. (2020) use multi-criteria decision analysis including erosion to identify SWC priority areas in China. Al-Shamiri & Ziadat (2012) monetise the erosion control benefit of WH in Yemen. [archetype: Soil conservation benefits compound over time — soils that erode are lost permanently; prevention costs far less than rehabilitation.]

effect_mechanism: Percolation tanks, check dams, and farm ponds in high-recharge-potential zones allow stored surface water to percolate into shallow aquifers, raising groundwater tables. This sustains dry-season well and spring yields, providing water for livestock and domestic use beyond the direct irrigation command area. Recharge benefits extend spatially beyond the structure footprint.
conditionality: Recharge effect requires permeable geology and favourable soil conditions. Clay-rich formations or impermeable bedrock limit subsurface recharge despite surface storage. Mussa et al. (2020) map groundwater recharge zones specifically to target percolation tank placement.
justification: Singh et al. (2016) identify prospective sites specifically for water harvesting and artificial groundwater recharge as combined objectives. Mussa et al. (2020) use hydrogeological delineation to prioritise recharge zones. Jha et al. (2014) include percolation tank siting explicitly for groundwater recharge.

effect_mechanism: WH structures convert episodic rainfall into stored water accessible during dry spells and drought years. Farm ponds, check dams, and cisterns provide 30-90 days of additional water supply beyond the rainy season. In drought years, stored water can sustain minimum crop production or livestock survival where rain-dependent farming would fail completely. This is the primary climate adaptation function of the NbS.
conditionality: Drought mitigation proportional to storage volume relative to command area water demand. Multi-year droughts can exhaust storage. Evaporation losses (especially in hot, windy arid environments) can reach 30-50% of stored volume. Maximum benefit in semi-arid zones (MAP 250-600mm) where drought is frequent but rainfall is sufficient to fill storage.
justification: This is the primary climate benefit of WH — confirmed across the vast majority of all 85 stocktake papers. hc et al. (2020) prioritise WH ponds specifically for drought from climate variability. Mahmoud et al. (2014) target the drought-prone Somali and Borena zones. Buraihi & Shariff (2015) assess future drought risk scenarios to justify WH site selection.

effect_mechanism: WH structures intercept and store surface runoff that would otherwise contribute to downstream flooding. Check dams and contour bunds slow peak flow velocity and extend travel time, reducing downstream flood peaks. Landscape-scale WH investment can meaningfully reduce flash flood risk in small to medium watersheds. However, overtopped or failed structures can temporarily increase flood risk.
conditionality: Flood mitigation effect requires landscape-scale deployment; individual structures have negligible effect on major flood events. Structure siting must avoid floodplain positions where inundation would damage or overtop them. Al-Hasani et al. (2023) model flood regulation as an explicit ecosystem-service output of WH.
justification: Abd-el-Kader et al. (2023) identify WH and flood mitigation as simultaneous spatial objectives in arid regions. Al-Hasani et al. (2023) quantify flood regulation benefits explicitly. Ejegu & Yegizaw (2020) model flood susceptibility as both a hazard to structures and a co-benefit driver for WH siting.

effect_mechanism: WH structures require upfront investment in earthworks, masonry, or lining materials. Costs range from near-zero for simple contour bunds using community labour, to significant engineering costs for check dams with concrete spillways or lined farm ponds. Unlike agroforestry, there are no ongoing input costs for tree seedlings or maintenance equivalent — the primary cost is construction.
conditionality: Costs are highly variable by structure type. Government subsidy schemes in India (MGNREGS) and Ethiopia significantly reduce effective smallholder costs. Remote locations increase material transport costs substantially.
economic_value_range: {"low_usd_ha_yr":50,"high_usd_ha_yr":800,"source_note":"Low: community labour contour bunds (~$50/ha). High: engineered farm ponds with liners and spillways (~$800/ha). Ibrahim et al. (2019) apply explicit cost-minimisation in dam site selection. Terencio et al. (2017) calculate EUR/m3 investment cost ratios for RWH in Portugal."}
justification: Ibrahim et al. (2019) apply cost-minimisation as a decision rule for dam site selection, explicitly minimising dam cross-section length to reduce costs. Terencio et al. (2017) estimate capital and O&M costs per m3 of storage. Ahmed et al. (2023) monetise ecosystem-service benefits in USD to construct a cost-benefit narrative. [archetype: Quick_returns archetype: upfront construction cost recovered within 1-3 seasons through improved yield stability. No long payback period unlike long_horizon agroforestry investments.]
